title: Iterative warm-start optimization with quantum imaginary time evolution
authors: Phillip C. Lotshaw, Titus Morris, Stuart Hadfield, Ryan Bennink
abstract: Approximate combinatorial optimization is a promising use case for quantum computers. The quantum optimization algorithms often employ a fixed ansatz that evolves an unbiased initial state towards states with better values of the optimand, then samples the states to determine an approximately optimal solution. However, promising alternative approaches have considered ``warm-start" and sampling-based methods that instead begin from the best known solution, which can be directly optimized with the quantum computer and updated as new information becomes available, potentially outperforming the fixed ansätze. Here we use these ideas to design a nonvariational quantum algorithm for combinatorial optimization. At each step the algorithm begins with a state superposed around the best known solution, then drives it to lower energy using quantum imaginary time evolution. These nonvariational, initial-state-dependent circuits are determined using analytic equations that are evaluated using only a conventional computer. After implementing the circuits, the state is sampled, potentially obtaining a new best-known solution to use as the initial state at the next iteration. Using simulations of the algorithm solving MaxCut on 3-regular graphs with 30 or fewer vertices and a shot budget of 100 total shots, the approach obtains median solutions within 95\% of the global optimum and finds optimal solutions in 11\% or more of cases, significantly outperforming random and simplified classical search procedures. We discuss several future directions.
summary: This paper proposes a new non-variational quantum algorithm for solving combinatorial optimization problems like Max-Cut. Instead of using a fixed ansatz, the algorithm uses an iterative 'warm-start' approach where each step begins with a state centered around the best solution found so far. By applying Quantum Imaginary Time Evolution, the algorithm drives the state toward lower energy levels. Simulations show that this method is highly efficient, finding near-optimal or exact solutions even with a very small number of quantum measurements.
main_problem: Efficiently approximating NP-hard combinatorial optimization problems, specifically Max-Cut, using quantum algorithms with limited shot budgets and circuit depth.
main_result: The iterative warm-start QITE algorithm achieves median solutions within 95% of the global optimum and finds exact optimal solutions in 11% of cases for 3-regular graphs with up to 30 vertices, significantly outperforming random and classical search methods.
method: A non-variational, iterative algorithm that uses Quantum Imaginary Time Evolution (QITE) to evolve a state initialized near the best-known solution. The circuit parameters are determined classically using analytic equations to avoid quantum device queries during the derivation phase.
model_or_system: The study focuses on the unweighted Max-Cut problem on 3-regular graphs, modeled by a Hamiltonian consisting of Pauli Z-Z interaction terms.
key_observables: Normalized cost (relative to the global optimum) and the probability of finding the exact optimal solution.
important_parameters: Shot budget (tested at 100 total shots), number of iterations (N_it = 10), imaginary time step (delta_tau = 0.1), and the initial state superposition parameter (phi) which varies from pi/4 to 0.
main_assumptions: The algorithm assumes that the circuit parameters can be computed analytically for product states and that the problem can be effectively represented by a pairwise ansatz.
figures_summary: Figure 1 provides a schematic of the classical-quantum hybrid loop; Figure 2 shows the median normalized cost and the fraction of optimal solutions found compared to other search methods.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of combinatorial optimization, describes the proposed iterative warm-start QITE algorithm and its classical-quantum workflow, presents numerical simulation results on 3-regular graphs, and concludes with discussions on limitations and future research directions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. The iterative quantum optimization algorithm uses a conventional computer (left) to compute a circuit that attempts to decrease the energy of the best-known solution, then sends the circuit to a quantum computer (right) which executes the circuit to update the best-known solution.

Fig. 2. Performance of the protocol for MaxCut on collections random 3-regular graphs. (top) Using 100 total measurements the approach yields median normalized cost values that are close to optimal for N ≤30, outperforming random search and a ”classical” version of the protocol that omits the quantum imaginary time evolution. Error bars show quartiles of the distributions. (bottom) The algorithm obtains significant optimal solution probabilities out to N = 30, while the classical and random searches do not.
Fig. 3. Performance at N = 22 as the number of iterations Nit and the number of shots per iteration Nshots/it are varied, with a fixed shot budget Nshots = 100.
Fig. 4. Performance at N = 22 as the number of shots Nshots is varied.
